### II.1 Multipolar AI pathway

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** No single system more impactful than all the others combined. Two worldviews share the branch and differ on the driver (human actors vs misaligned subsystems) and on the evidentiary axis.
:::

Here catastrophe does not require one system winning; it arrives from the interaction of many. Post 2 split this branch three ways: competitive (systems or states in territorial opposition), cooperative (sub-critical systems conspiring against us), and a human variant (nations spooked by each other's machines). The two worldviews below cover the competitive and the human; the cooperative case, many AIs actively colluding, is a real branch with no clear champion. They differ on what actually drives the failure.

**Multipolar Racing.** Even well-intentioned, individually rational actors can produce catastrophe together, no rogue optimizer required. Everyone in the frontier race claims to prefer slower, more careful development, but because no lab can unilaterally enforce a slowdown, sitting out means ceding control to competitors they trust less than themselves. Anthropic frames its own existence as a calculated bet on exactly this logic, and DeepMind reasons similarly. The dominant danger is unsupervised competition: state militaries, corporate labs, and open-source advocates in a game-theoretic trap where coordination is functionally impossible and racing is rewarded, and catastrophe arrives through bargaining failures, the erosion of human control, and models shipped before they are vetted because a competitor shipped first. This is advanced by [Andrew Critch](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/Kobbt3nQgv3yn29pr/my-motivation-and-theory-of-change-for-working-in-ai), [Helen Toner](https://cset.georgetown.edu/), [Stuart Russell](https://www.amazon.com/Human-Compatible-Artificial-Intelligence-Problem/dp/0525558616), and [Toby Ord](https://theprecipice.com/). Critch assigns around 85 percent probability of extinction over roughly 25 years, about 35 percent via loss-of-control events and around 50 percent via "industrial dehumanization," humans losing economic and political relevance in an unsteerable machine economy, explicitly not via a sovereign AGI. Their epistemology is political-economy-first: the load-bearing facts are institutional and game-theoretic, not facts about any one model's internals. A perfectly aligned AI still kills you if the firm that builds it is out-competed and absorbed by an adversary wielding an unaligned one. What falsifies it: durable international governance that structurally changes the incentives, not voluntary commitments.

**Structural Disempowerment.** The control problem is deep and general; fast takeoff is one way for it to end badly, slow takeoff another. Imagine markets run by high-frequency algorithms, routed through autonomous logistics, drawing power from grid-balancing AI. No single node decides to eradicate humanity, yet the economic penalty for swapping these optimized systems back to slower human oversight grows so steep that human intervention is priced out of existence. A series of sufficiently capable systems with goals slightly off from human welfare gradually accrete power until human control is administrative, a title for decisions already made elsewhere. The argument rests on orthogonality and instrumental convergence, and [Joe Carlsmith](https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.13353) and [John Wentworth](https://www.lesswrong.com/users/johnswentworth) articulate it, building on the [Gradual Disempowerment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.16946) framework. Unlike Racing, the driver is not human competition but the slow boil of handing infrastructure to misaligned optimizers; and unlike Racing, the epistemology is prior-dominant, deductions about optimization rather than extrapolations from how today's models behave. The intervention is upstream: formal alignment guarantees before integration, not after the infrastructure has priced out the alternative.

::: {.aside}
Same branch, different engine. Both reject the single-optimizer story and live here, but Racing is driven by human actors in a coordination trap and updates on institutional facts, while Structural Disempowerment is driven by misaligned subsystems accreting power and reasons from structural priors. They look adjacent and share almost no evidentiary standard, which is exactly the kind of hidden non-overlap this map exists to surface.
:::
